source: All-In Podcast — DRAM bottleneck, AI infrastructure, Chinese open source models
summary: Micron's new supply agreements with floor pricing above prior cycle gross margin peaks are potentially transformational for the DRAM industry
summary_extended: Baker argues that Micron's newly announced supply and demand agreements (SDAs) represent a potentially transformational structural shift in the DRAM industry's business model. Covering approximately 50% of Micron's revenue with just four customers, these contracts include both floor and ceiling pricing mechanisms — critically, the floor pricing alone exceeds the gross margin peaks achieved during prior DRAM upcycles. Baker views this as a fundamental improvement in DRAM pricing stability and profitability that the market has not yet fully priced into Micron's valuation. The investment implication is that Micron's earnings floor is structurally higher than historical cycles would suggest, making the stock potentially undervalued on a through-cycle basis. Baker describes the development as 'pretty maybe end up being very transformational for the industry,' signaling high conviction in the medium-term thesis.
detail: Micron announced supply agreements (SDAs) covering ~50% of revenue with just four customers, with floor and ceiling pricing. The floor pricing in these contracts exceeds prior cycle peak gross margins, which Baker views as a structural improvement in the DRAM business model that the market has not fully priced.
confidence_evidence: Baker describes this as 'pretty maybe end up being very transformational for the industry' and provides specific contract details
quote_fragment: floor pricing in these new contracts is ahead of prior cycle peaks from a gross margin perspective

ticker_audit.reasoning: The thesis is specifically about Micron's supply agreements and their transformational impact on Micron's gross margins and business model. MU is the direct subject of this thesis.
